particles.usage: Marks the subject, often for new information, emphasis, or with 있다/없다.
particles.notes: Often emphasises who or what does the action.

particles.usage: Marks the sentence topic, often with contrast or known information.

examples.en: As for me, I am a student.

particles.notes: Can imply contrast depending on the context.

particles.usage: Marks the direct object of a verb.
particles.notes: Can be dropped in casual speech, but keep it in writing and in strict practice.

particles.usage: Shows possession or relation.
particles.notes: Often omitted in casual speech.

particles.usage: Marks destination, time, or static location with existence.
particles.notes: Use with 있다/없다 for existence.

particles.usage: Marks where an action happens, or where movement starts from.
particles.notes: 에 is destination/static location; 에서 is action location.

particles.usage: Marks means, direction, method, or material.

examples.en: I go to school by bus.

particles.notes: Use 로 after a vowel or ㄹ batchim.

particles.usage: Marks the recipient or target person.

examples.en: I gave it to the teacher.

particles.notes: 한테 is casual, 에게 neutral, 께 honorific.

particles.usage: Marks the source person.

examples.en: I got a letter from my friend.

particles.notes: Use for people or animals, not locations.

particles.usage: Connects nouns or marks accompaniment.

examples.en: I went to the market with Chulsoo.

particles.notes: 와/과 more formal, 하고 neutral, (이)랑 casual.

particles.usage: Adds inclusion and can replace another particle position.
particles.notes: Attach 도 directly where the particle slot belongs.

particles.usage: Restricts meaning to only that noun.
particles.notes: Attach directly after the noun.

particles.usage: Used with a negative form to mean there is nothing except or no choice but.

examples.en: I have no choice but to study.

particles.notes: Must pair with a negative predicate or negative meaning.

particles.usage: Marks the starting point and ending point in time or space.

examples.en: I work from morning until evening.

particles.usage: Shows similarity or likeness.
Compares degree or amount.

examples.en: I worked as hard as you.

particles.usage: Marks the comparison standard.
Use 은 after consonants and 는 after vowels. It marks topic or contrast.

examples.en: As for me, I am a student.

particles.notes: Use 은 after consonants and 는 after vowels. It marks topic or contrast.

particles.usage: Use 이 after consonants and 가 after vowels. It marks the subject or new information.
particles.notes: Use 이 after consonants and 가 after vowels. It marks the subject or new information.

particles.usage: Use 을 after consonants and 를 after vowels. It marks the object, the thing receiving the action or feeling.
particles.notes: Use 을 after consonants and 를 after vowels. It marks the object, the thing receiving the action or feeling.

particles.usage: Use 에 for destination or time. 카페에 가요 means go to the cafe. 두 시에 means at two o'clock.
particles.notes: Use 에 for destination or time. 카페에 가요 means go to the cafe. 두 시에 means at two o'clock.

particles.usage: Use 에서 for where an action happens. 카페에서 공부해요 means study at the cafe.
particles.notes: Use 에서 for where an action happens. 카페에서 공부해요 means study at the cafe.

particles.usage: 의 marks possession like of or apostrophe-s. In casual Korean, 의 is often dropped.
particles.notes: 의 marks possession like of or apostrophe-s. In casual Korean, 의 is often dropped.

particles.usage: 도 means also or too. It can replace 은/는, 이/가, or 을/를 depending on context.
particles.notes: 도 means also or too. It can replace 은/는, 이/가, or 을/를 depending on context.

particles.usage: Use 와 after vowel endings and 과 after consonant endings to connect nouns. It is a bit more written/formal than 하고.
particles.notes: Use 와 after vowel endings and 과 after consonant endings to connect nouns. It is a bit more written/formal than 하고.

particles.usage: 하고 connects nouns and is common in speech.

examples.en: I go to the cafe with a friend.

particles.notes: 하고 connects nouns and is common in speech.

particles.usage: Use 이랑 after consonants and 랑 after vowels. It is casual spoken Korean.

examples.en: I watch a movie with Noona.

particles.notes: Use 이랑 after consonants and 랑 after vowels. It is casual spoken Korean.

particles.usage: 한테 marks the person receiving something or the direction of an action.

examples.en: I give a gift to Noona.

particles.notes: 한테 marks the person receiving something or the direction of an action.

particles.usage: 에게 is like 한테 but a little more written/formal.

examples.en: I ask the teacher a question.

particles.notes: 에게 is like 한테 but a little more written/formal.

particles.usage: 께 is the honorific version of 한테/에게.

examples.en: I give it to the teacher.

particles.notes: 께 is the honorific version of 한테/에게.

particles.usage: 한테서 or 에게서 marks the person something comes from.

examples.en: I received a gift from a friend.

particles.notes: 한테서 or 에게서 marks the person something comes from.

particles.usage: 부터 marks the starting point in time or sequence.
particles.notes: 부터 marks the starting point in time or sequence.

particles.usage: 까지 marks the end point in time or place.
particles.notes: 까지 marks the end point in time or place.

particles.usage: 에서부터 marks the starting place.

examples.en: I walk from school to home.

particles.notes: 에서부터 marks the starting place.

particles.usage: Use 으로 after consonants and 로 after vowels or ㄹ. It can mean by means of transportation/tool or toward a direction.
particles.notes: Use 으로 after consonants and 로 after vowels or ㄹ. It can mean by means of transportation/tool or toward a direction.

particles.usage: 만 means only.
particles.notes: 만 means only.

particles.usage: 밖에 is usually used with a negative verb to mean only/nothing but.
particles.notes: 밖에 is usually used with a negative verb to mean only/nothing but.

particles.usage: 마다 means every or each.
particles.notes: 마다 means every or each.

particles.usage: 보다 after a noun means than for comparison.

examples.en: I like tea more than coffee.

particles.notes: 보다 after a noun means than for comparison.

particles.usage: 처럼 means like or as if.
particles.notes: 처럼 means like or as if.

particles.usage: 같이 means together with or like.

examples.en: I study together with a friend.

particles.notes: 같이 means together with or like.

grammar.title: Present progressive
formation: Take the verb stem.
Add -고 있어요.
Use it for an action in progress right now.
pitfalls: Do not mix this up with the plain present if the lesson specifically targets an action in progress.
Keep object particles in strict mode.

grammar.title: No choice but to
grammar.meaning: have no choice but to; cannot help but
formation: Take the verb stem.
Attach -(으)ㄹ 수밖에 없어요.
Use it when circumstances leave no other option.

examples.en: Because I have an exam, I have no choice but to study.

pitfalls: Do not treat this as the same as simple ability or possibility.
The 밖에 here contributes the meaning of there being no other alternative.

grammar.title: Place-time-object-action sentence pattern
grammar.meaning: a beginner blueprint for describing where, when, and what you do
formation: Use 에서 for place of action.
Use 에 for time.
Use 을/를 for the direct object.
Finish with a polite verb.

examples.ko: 저는 도서관에서 세 시에 책을 읽어요.
examples.en: I read a book at the library at 3 o'clock.
examples.audioText: 저는 도서관에서 세 시에 책을 읽어요.

pitfalls: Do not use 에 for the action location.
Keep the object particle when the target pattern includes it.

grammar.title: Go to see; go to do
grammar.meaning: go in order to do; come in order to do
formation: Take the verb stem and attach -러.
Follow with 가요 or 와요.

examples.en: I go to the cafe to see a friend.

pitfalls: The person you are going to see still takes 을/를.

grammar.title: 고
formation: Attach 고 to a verb stem to connect actions. It can mean and or and then.

examples.en: I eat and then watch a movie.

grammar.notes: Attach 고 to a verb stem to connect actions. It can mean and or and then.

grammar.title: ~하고 있어요
grammar.meaning: be doing, am doing something right now
formation: Use verb stem + 고 있어요 to describe an action happening right now. 공부하다 becomes 공부하고 있어요.
grammar.notes: Use verb stem + 고 있어요 to describe an action happening right now. 공부하다 becomes 공부하고 있어요.

grammar.title: 러/으러 가다
grammar.meaning: go in order to do something
formation: Use 러 after a vowel-ending stem and 으러 after a consonant-ending stem. 보다 becomes 보러 가요 because 보 ends in a vowel.

examples.en: I go to the cafe to see Noona.

grammar.notes: Use 러 after a vowel-ending stem and 으러 after a consonant-ending stem. 보다 becomes 보러 가요 because 보 ends in a vowel.

grammar.title: 아/어요
formation: Use 아요 after stems with 아 or 오. Use 어요 after most other vowels. 하다 becomes 해요.
grammar.notes: Use 아요 after stems with 아 or 오. Use 어요 after most other vowels. 하다 becomes 해요.

grammar.title: 았/었어요
formation: Use 았어요 after bright vowels like 아 or 오. Use 었어요 after most others. 하다 becomes 했어요.
grammar.notes: Use 았어요 after bright vowels like 아 or 오. Use 었어요 after most others. 하다 becomes 했어요.

grammar.title: ㄹ/을 거예요
formation: Use ㄹ 거예요 after vowel stems and 을 거예요 after consonant stems. It means will or going to.
grammar.notes: Use ㄹ 거예요 after vowel stems and 을 거예요 after consonant stems. It means will or going to.

grammar.title: 고 싶어요
formation: Attach 고 싶어요 to a verb stem to say want to do.
grammar.notes: Attach 고 싶어요 to a verb stem to say want to do.

grammar.title: 지 않아요
formation: Attach 지 않아요 to a verb stem to make a formal negative.
grammar.notes: Attach 지 않아요 to a verb stem to make a formal negative.

grammar.title: 안
formation: Put 안 before many verbs for a simple negative.
grammar.notes: Put 안 before many verbs for a simple negative.

grammar.title: 못
formation: Put 못 before many verbs to mean cannot do because of ability or circumstance.
grammar.notes: Put 못 before many verbs to mean cannot do because of ability or circumstance.

grammar.title: 주세요
grammar.meaning: please give, please do for me
formation: Use 주세요 to request something or ask someone to do something for you.
grammar.notes: Use 주세요 to request something or ask someone to do something for you.

grammar.title: 세요
formation: 세요 is used to politely ask or respectfully describe someone doing something.
grammar.notes: 세요 is used to politely ask or respectfully describe someone doing something.

grammar.title: 아/어도 돼요
grammar.meaning: may I, it is okay to
formation: Use 아/어도 돼요 to say it is okay to do something.
grammar.notes: Use 아/어도 돼요 to say it is okay to do something.

grammar.title: 아/어야 해요
formation: Use 아/어야 해요 to say have to or must do something.
grammar.notes: Use 아/어야 해요 to say have to or must do something.

grammar.title: 수밖에 없어요
formation: Use verb stem + ㄹ/을 수밖에 없어요. It means there is no choice except doing that action. Normal spelling is 수밖에.

examples.en: I have no choice but to study.

grammar.notes: Use verb stem + ㄹ/을 수밖에 없어요. It means there is no choice except doing that action. Normal spelling is 수밖에.

grammar.title: 때문에
formation: Noun + 때문에 means because of the noun. Verb/adjective clause + 기 때문에 means because.

examples.en: I cannot go because of rain.

grammar.notes: Noun + 때문에 means because of the noun. Verb/adjective clause + 기 때문에 means because.

grammar.title: 갔다가
grammar.meaning: go and then, did and then
formation: Verb + 았/었다가 shows doing one action and then another, often returning or changing state.

examples.en: I go to the cafe and then go home.

grammar.notes: Verb + 았/었다가 shows doing one action and then another, often returning or changing state.

grammar.title: [명사]은/는 [몇 월] [며칠]이에요
formation: Use this to say when an event is.
grammar.notes: Use this to say when an event is.

grammar.title: [장소]에서 [시간]에 [목적어]를/을 [동사]아요/어요
formation: Use 에서 for the place of action, 에 for time, 을/를 for object, then the polite verb.

examples.ko: 저는 카페에서 오후 두 시에 커피를 마셔요.
examples.en: I drink coffee at the cafe at 2 PM.
examples.audioText: 저는 카페에서 오후 두 시에 커피를 마셔요.

grammar.notes: Use 에서 for the place of action, 에 for time, 을/를 for object, then the polite verb.

rules: Prefer stems where both are superficially plausible.
All options must be noun+particle chunks.
Use with destination/static-location vs action-location contexts.
Use with give/receive/ask contexts involving people.
밖에 options should usually be paired with negative meaning in explanations.
Use when the learner is comparing similarity, degree, or standard.
All options should be same-verb nearby beginner forms.

prompt.stem: Choose the best Korean for “I am studying Korean right now.”
prompt.stemKo: “I am studying Korean right now.”에 가장 알맞은 한국어를 고르세요.

choices.why: Progressive action in progress right now.
Possible in some contexts, but not the best target for an action happening right now in this lesson.
Past tense.
Means going in order to study, not currently studying.

exercises.explanation: The target meaning is a current action in progress, so V-고 있어요 is the best match.
exercises.naturalnessNote: The plain present can sometimes describe habits or near-future intention, but this drill targets progressive aspect.

choices.why: 에 is not the action-location particle here.
공부하다 is an action, so use 에서 for where the action happens.
으로 is direction or means.
까지 marks an end point.

exercises.explanation: 에서 marks the place where an action happens.

prompt.stem: Which option best completes the idea “I only have 1,000 won left”?

choices.why: 도 means also, not only.
This wording is not the intended meaning.
밖에 + negative gives the target meaning of only/nothing but.
보다 is comparison, not restriction.

exercises.explanation: 밖에 is used with a negative predicate to express ‘only / nothing but’.
exercises.particleNote: 밖에 usually needs a negative form such as 안, 못, 없어요.

prompt.stem: Choose the best chunk for “as hard as you.”

choices.why: 보다 means than.
만큼 means as much/as...as.
처럼 means like, similar to.
까지 means until/to.

exercises.explanation: 만큼 matches equal degree: as hard as you.

prompt.stem: Fill the blank: 친구___ 편지를 받았어요.

choices.why: 에게 marks the recipient or target person.
에게서 marks the source person you received something from.
에 is not used for source person here.
으로 marks means or direction.

exercises.explanation: 받다 takes a source person with 에게서/한테서.

prompt.stem: What does 공부할 수밖에 없어요 mean?

choices.why: That would be 공부하고 싶어요.
That would be 공부할 수 있어요.

choices.text: I have no choice but to study.
choices.why: This construction expresses unavoidable necessity.
That would require past tense.

exercises.explanation: V-(으)ㄹ 수밖에 없어요 expresses that circumstances leave no alternative.
Use 에서 for the location where the action happens.
The target lesson form is V-고 있어요 for an action in progress.
Use 에게/한테 for the person receiving the gift.
exercises.particleNote: 께 is possible if the recipient is someone you speak to honorifically.

exercises.explanation: Use 부터 for the starting point and 까지 for the end point.
This completes the unavoidable-necessity construction.

prompt.stem: Build: “I read a book at the library at 3 o'clock.”
prompt.audioText: 저는 도서관에서 세 시에 책을 읽어요.

exercises.targetMeaning: I read a book at the library at 3 o'clock.

strict: 저는 도서관에서 세 시에 책을 읽어요.
relaxed: 저는 도서관에서 세 시에 책 읽어요.

exercises.modelAnswer: 저는 도서관에서 세 시에 책을 읽어요.
exercises.explanation: 에서 marks action location, 에 marks time, and 을 marks the direct object.

exercises.explanation: Use topic + time adverb + object + progressive form.

강아지 is the thing being liked, so it uses the object marker. 강아지 ends in a vowel, so use 를.
고양이 is the object of 좋아해요. It ends in a vowel, so use 를.
에 marks destination. You are going to the cafe.
에서 marks where the action happens. Studying happens at the cafe.
누나 is the person being seen, so it takes 를.
보러 가요 means going to see. 보 ends in a vowel, so use 러.
먹 ends in a consonant, so use 으러.
오전 means AM. 오후 means PM.
오후 means PM. 오전 means AM.
먹 does not have 아 or 오, so use 어요.
공부하다 becomes 공부하고 있어요. Structure: verb stem + 고 있어요.
먹다 becomes 먹고 있어요.
자다 becomes 자고 있어요.
읽다 becomes 읽고 있어요.
듣다 becomes 듣고 있어요.

prompt.audioText: 내일 시험이 있어서 공부할 수밖에 없어요.

exercises.explanation: 수밖에 없어요 means there is no choice but to do it. Standard spelling is usually 수밖에.
Dates use 이에요 after the date phrase.

prompt.stem: 저는 카페___ 오후 두 시___ 커피를 마셔요.
prompt.audioText: 저는 카페에서 에 오후 두 시에서 에 커피를 마셔요.

exercises.explanation: Use 에서 for where the action happens and 에 for the time.
한테/에게 marks the person receiving something.
한테서/에게서 means from someone.
부터 marks the start and 까지 marks the end.
Use 로 after a vowel or ㄹ ending. 버스 ends in a vowel sound, so 버스로 가요.
만 means only.
밖에 is used with a negative verb to mean nothing but/only.
때문에 means because of.
갔다가 means go and then do another action.
For a simple beginner sentence, 강아지 is the object of 좋아해요, so 강아지를 is better.
에 marks destination. 에서 marks where an action happens. Since 가요 means go, use 카페에.
먹다 becomes 먹어요 because the vowel is not 아 or 오.
Remove 다 to get the stem 보, then add 러 because 보 ends in a vowel.
Hours use 시. Minutes use 분. So 2:30 PM is 오후 두 시 삼십 분.
Use verb stem + 고 있어요. 공부하다 becomes 공부하고 있어요.
Use verb stem + ㄹ/을 수밖에 없어요. 공부하다 becomes 공부할 수밖에 없어요.
The person receiving something takes 한테/에게, not 를.
For time starting point, use 부터. 에서 is for action location or starting place.
밖에 usually needs a negative verb. 커피밖에 없어요 means there is only coffee.
Use 로 after a vowel or ㄹ. 버스 becomes 버스로.
가다 loses 다 and becomes 가요.
먹 uses 어요 because it does not have 아 or 오.
보 + 아요 contracts to 봐요.
하다 becomes 해요.
Use stem 공부하 + 고 있어요.
Use stem 먹 + 고 있어요.
Use stem 읽 + 고 있어요.

보 ends in a vowel, so use 러.
